# Football Transfer Fee Analysis

Thomas Pequegnot

Galal Bichara

## Background

### General Information

This project audits a baseline random forest model that predicts football player transfer fees using historical transfer records from 2000-2018 alongside FIFA video game attributes. We extend the original analysis by rigorously evaluating the model for geographic and systemic biases, specifically applying fairness metrics across demographic axes like player nationality and current league. By quantifying the tension between raw predictive precision and equitable valuation, the audit highlights areas of potential disparate impact within the algorithm and ultimately, this framework provides actionable insights for developing more objective, transparent, and fair player assessment tools in sports analytics.

### Purpose and Goals

The purpose of the original solution is to be able to be able to predict transfer fees from attributes of players pulled from FIFA. The purpose of the ADS is to better account for bias and produce a solution that is more interpretable on a sub level. Instead of just taking features and feeding it into a random forest model, we want to see how the model reacts to changes in certain features. For instance, we want to see if demographic features such as country of birth or the league they are being transferred to have a strong influence on transfer fees and produce a result that is more interpretable for a user.  
We have also seen a shift in the perspective of using data in football, with more and more football clubs embracing bigger data teams. As a result, we think that models like this will be used to determine player prices in the future. To prevent the same cycle of dominance and the same clubs remaining in poverty, we want to ensure fairness among players and check that players of different demographics are treated fairly. This ADS directly connects to course themes of proxy discrimination, fairness metrics, and the tradeoff between model accuracy and equitable outcomes

## Input and Output

### Dataset Description

#### Transfer Fee Dataset

This dataset contains the transfer fees for players between the years 2000 and 2018\. Specifically, this dataset only covers the top 250 transfers per season, thus not all the transfers made in this timeframe were recorded. The dataset is from Kaggle and there are 4700 total rows and 10 columns in this dataset. The source of this dataset isn’t specified anywhere in the description on Kaggle, however it likely came from Transfermarkt.

#### FIFA Dataset

This is a series of datasets from the video game FIFA. The dataset contains players, their overall ratings, and specific attribute analytics for each player (how fast the game rates them, their shooting rating, passing rating, etc.). There is some bias in the dataset as the game rates players themselves and not from objective measures, but for the most part, FIFA has done a good job rating players accurately. The biggest issue to consider in this dataset is that only FIFA 15-18 were used in the original solution so it is difficult to incorporate FIFA attributes into our ADS if we want our solution to generalize prior to 2015 (unless we pull from other sources). The source of this dataset was from sofifa (publicly available website displaying FIFA stats) and the owner of the dataset scraped off this website. The dataset has 104 columns and 15465 players, however we won’t use all columns and rows as not all players were transferred, and not all columns are relevant for our ADS. We also want to see if it is possible to scrape and incorporate wage data, both raw and done as a percentile of the squad. We know that wages are a good indicator of a squad’s wealth, meaning they are less willing to sell players due to their financial well standing. It is also an indicator that shows a player’s importance, if they are earning a higher wage than the rest of the squad.

The above chart (Ability-Fee correlation) reveals that the relationship between player ability and transfer fee varies significantly by nationality, with Scotland, Argentina, and Senegal showing negative correlations, which suggests that for certain nationalities higher ability does not translate to higher fees.

![][image16]

### System Output

The system involves a RandomForest Regressor to predict transfer fees derived from player attributes. Since this is a regression problem, the evaluation metrics used were Mean Squared Error and R^2. The original solution did not incorporate fairness metrics, but since our ADS attempts to factor multiple demographics and measures true impact of certain features, we will use fairness metrics such as demographic parity ratio. We also want to check if certain features were proxy variables for demographics (nation, race, league- to some extent). We also want to check if certain groups are over and underpredicted so we want to properly measure and factor those with fairness metrics.

## Preliminary Performance analysis

The baseline Random Forest model achieves an R² of 80.0%, but this is largely driven by market value — dropping it collapses accuracy to \~44%, meaning the model is mostly learning a the relationship between market value and transfer fee, rather than using the stats that determine a player’s ability. The RMSE of \~6.92 million euros is also significant because most transfers are valued at less than 25 million, but is exacerbated by outliers like Neymar and Mbappé. Combined with the model only covering 2015–2018 FIFA data and losing \~27% of records in cleaning, these results confirm the model works but has clear structural weaknesses our audit will address

## Preliminary Observation

The dataset only covers the top 250 transfers per season, so it skews heavily toward expensive deals and elite clubs, with a small number of nationalities dominating the records. This means players from underrepresented regions — Africa, Asia, smaller European leagues — will likely have higher prediction errors since the model has seen little data on them. The FIFA attribute data only covers 2015–2018, leaving a significant gap for earlier transfers. Features like league and FIFA ratings also act as proxies for nationality, meaning even without explicitly including nationality, the model can still produce biased predictions through those variables. Overall, the baseline model likely performs well on aggregate metrics while quietly producing unfair valuations for certain player groups

## Detailed Plan

### Phase 1

Before building our ADS, we want to first properly understand the original solution and establish what the baseline evaluation metrics are. This is done by thoroughly inspecting the original solution’s code, and reading through the documentation done via comments and markdown text on their analysis. From there, we highlight potential flaws in the system, any inefficiencies in the code structure, and undergo deep EDA through plots and statistics to understand any overlooked relationships between different variables. 

### Phase 2

Here we select the features we plan to further investigate and account for in our ADS. We selected these through domain knowledge and intentionally selected variables that we know impacts transfer fees and weren’t accounted for in the original solution. The features we selected are nationality, league transferred to, age, contract length, wage (in euros), and potential. Then we see if there are any features that we can also derive from the features we have selected.

### Phase 3

In Phase 3 we want to design our fairness metric framework. We begin by defining our fairness attributes for each feature and assessing which fairness metrics best apply. We re-train the Random Forest model with fairness-aware techniques and evaluate performance-fairness trade-offs. We explore in-processing approaches: fairness-constrained optimization using libraries such as Fairlearn, and evaluate the model with both traditional and fairness metrics. 

### Phase 4

We want to show the interpretation of the model so we produce Partial Dependence Plots to showcase gaps in transfer fees depending on the feature, and incorporate LIME to ensure our ADS is fair on a granular level. We use our domain expertise to inspect more controversial predictions and output the weights for those decisions. After model interpretability, we produce our final audit report containing our technical and executive summaries, a presentation deck summarizing our findings and recommendations, and showcase our code repository with our notebooks containing logic behind our technical decisions while also including the original solution which we treated as our baseline.

## References

\[1\] V. Ghazaryan, "Top 250 Football Transfers from 2000 to 2018," Kaggle, 2019\. \[Online\]. Available: [https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/vardan95ghazaryan/top-250-football-transfers-from-2000-to-2018](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/vardan95ghazaryan/top-250-football-transfers-from-2000-to-2018)  
\[2\] S. Leone, "FIFA 20 Complete Player Dataset," Kaggle, 2020\. \[Online\]. Available: [https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/stefanoleone992/fifa-20-complete-player-dataset/data](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/stefanoleone992/fifa-20-complete-player-dataset/data)  
\[3\] stmy456, "Football Transfer Analysis," Kaggle Notebook, 2020\. \[Online\]. Available: [https://www.kaggle.com/code/stmy456/football-transfer-analysis](https://www.kaggle.com/code/stmy456/football-transfer-analysis)
